producer.name: Halmari Tea Estate
producer.history: Award-winning estate in Upper Assam. Known for rich, malty orthodox teas.

brewing.notes: Strong, malty, takes milk well. A breakfast tea. Can handle a longer steep without becoming unpleasant.

notes:
Full-bodied second flush Assam from Halmari estate. Malt, honey,
bread crust — the quintessential breakfast tea character. Strong enough
to stand up to milk and sugar, but rewarding black too. The reliable
morning starter in the collection.

photo.alt: Dark, twisted Assam orthodox tea leaves

producer.name: Fuding White Tea Co.
producer.history: Fuding in Fujian province is the birthplace of white tea. Traditional sun-withering process.

brewing.notes: White tea is forgiving. Can also cold-brew overnight for a different character — sweet and clean.

notes:
White Peony from Fuding — a mix of buds and young leaves, minimally
processed. Honey sweetness, hay-like aroma, delicate stone fruit notes.
Improves with age (white tea ages well). Currently drinking young and fresh,
but could be set aside for a year or two for more depth.

photo.alt: White and green Bai Mu Dan leaves with silver buds

producer.name: Uva Highlands Estate
producer.history: High-altitude estate in Sri Lanka's Uva province. The elevation and eastern monsoon winds create a distinctive menthol character.

brewing.notes: Standard Western brew. Takes milk if you want, but interesting enough black.

notes:
High-grown Ceylon from Uva province. Brisk, slightly mentholated,
with a crisp astringency that works well in the afternoon. Not the most
complex tea in the collection, but reliable and versatile. Considering
gifting — it's a crowd-pleaser without requiring specialized brewing knowledge.

photo.alt: Wiry black Ceylon tea leaves

producer.name: Wuyi Mountain Farm
producer.history: Wuyi mountains in Fujian, birthplace of rock oolongs. Mineral-rich volcanic soil gives the characteristic 'yan yun' (rock rhyme).

brewing.notes: Very short first steeps (10-15s), extending gradually. The minerality emerges on later steeps.

notes:
Wuyi rock oolong — heavily roasted, complex, with the distinctive mineral
backbone ('rock rhyme') that defines this style. Dark fruit, charcoal,
roasted nuts in the first steeps, transitioning to a sweet mineral finish.
Rewards patience and multiple infusions. A tea with genuine depth.

photo.alt: Dark twisted Da Hong Pao oolong leaves

producer.name: Makaibari
producer.history: World's first tea factory (1859). Pioneer of biodynamic tea cultivation in Darjeeling.

brewing.notes: Lower temperature than you'd expect for a 'black' tea. First flush is closer to oolong in character.

notes:
Spring first flush Darjeeling from Makaibari estate. Muscatel grape notes,
floral brightness, light body. More like a green tea than a typical black —
first flush Darjeeling is its own category. Drink fresh; it doesn't improve
with age. The champagne of teas, as the cliché goes (but it's true).

photo.alt: Light, wiry first flush Darjeeling leaves with green tips

producer.name: Rare Tea Company
producer.history: London-based, sources directly from estates. Known for whole-leaf quality in a category dominated by bags.

brewing.notes: Standard Western brewing. The bergamot is real oil, not artificial flavoring.

notes:
A proper Earl Grey — whole-leaf Chinese black tea with natural bergamot
oil. The base tea has enough body to carry the citrus without being
overwhelmed. A reminder that this classic can be much more than
the supermarket teabag version. Afternoon staple.

photo.alt: Dark tea leaves with visible bergamot oil sheen

producer.name: Ippodo
producer.history: Founded 1717 in Kyoto. Imperial warrant holder, supplying tea to the Japanese court for centuries.

brewing.notes: Hot water, quick steep. The toasted rice puffs up beautifully. Good with food.

notes:
Green tea blended with toasted rice from Ippodo. The rice adds a warm,
popcorn-like aroma and nutty flavor that complements the grassy base.
Unpretentious, satisfying, pairs well with meals. A daily-drinking tea
with zero pretension and genuine charm.

photo.alt: Ippodo genmaicha with visible toasted rice grains

producer.name: Kanbayashi
producer.history: Tea suppliers to the Tokugawa Shogunate. Centuries-old lineage from Uji.

brewing.notes: Low temperature is essential. First steep brings out umami; subsequent steeps reveal sweetness.

notes:
Exceptional gyokuro from Kanbayashi, historic Uji producer. Deep umami,
marine sweetness, almost broth-like in body. The kind of tea that
rewards full attention — not a daily driver, a meditation.

photo.alt: Sealed packet of Kanbayashi Gyokuro Zuigyoku

producer.name: Kanbayashi
producer.history: Tea suppliers to the Tokugawa Shogunate. Centuries-old lineage from Uji.

brewing.notes: More delicate than standard hojicha. Slightly lower temp brings out the residual umami from the gyokuro stems.

notes:
Premium hojicha made from roasted gyokuro stems (karigane). Carries a
deeper sweetness and residual umami that regular hojicha lacks. The
roasting is lighter, preserving some of the shaded character. A bridge
between the intensity of gyokuro and the comfort of hojicha.

photo.alt: Light brown roasted gyokuro stems

producer.name: Kanbayashi
producer.history: Tea suppliers to the Tokugawa Shogunate. Centuries-old lineage from Uji.

brewing.notes: Boiling water is fine. Quick steep for roasty sweetness. Good evening tea — low caffeine.

notes:
Roasted green tea from Kanbayashi. Warm, nutty, caramel-like sweetness
without bitterness. Comforting rather than complex. A perfect evening tea
or post-meal palate cleanser. The roasting process reduces caffeine significantly.

photo.alt: Brown roasted hojicha leaves from Kanbayashi

producer.name: Ippodo
producer.history: Founded 1717 in Kyoto. Imperial warrant holder, supplying tea to the Japanese court for centuries.

brewing.notes: Stems are forgiving — hard to over-steep. Good for casual brewing.

notes:
Twig tea from Ippodo — made from stems and stalks rather than leaves.
Light, sweet, slightly nutty with almost no astringency. Very low caffeine.
A gentle, easygoing tea for any time of day. The stalks give it a
distinctive appearance and a clean, uncomplicated character.

photo.alt: Pale green kukicha stems and twigs

producer.name: West Lake Tea Village
producer.history: Longjing village near Hangzhou. One of China's Ten Famous Teas, cultivated here since the Tang dynasty.

brewing.notes: Glass cup recommended to watch leaves unfurl. First steep is the sweetest. Don't over-steep.

notes:
Pre-Qingming (early spring) Dragon Well from the West Lake area.
Flat, pan-fired leaves with a chestnut aroma and sweet, vegetal flavor.
No astringency when brewed correctly. One of the great Chinese greens —
subtle, elegant, and best consumed young. Drink before spring ends.

photo.alt: Flat green Longjing tea leaves

brewing.notes: Simmer tea and spices in water+milk (1:1) for 5 minutes. Strain. Sweeten to taste. This is cooking, not infusion.

notes:
Personal masala chai blend — strong CTC Assam base with cardamom,
ginger, cinnamon, black pepper, cloves. Mixed at home to personal
ratios. The recipe changes slightly each time. A comforting,
spice-forward drink for cold mornings or afternoon crashes.

photo.alt: Chai spices and tea leaves in a mixing bowl

producer.name: Marukyu Koyamaen
producer.history: Founded 1704 in Uji. One of the most respected tea houses in Japan, seven generations of expertise.

brewing.notes: Good as daily matcha. Slightly more bitter than ceremonial grade — works well as latte base too.

notes:
Everyday-grade matcha from Marukyu Koyamaen. Not as refined as their
ceremonial offerings, but reliably good for daily preparation. Balanced
between bitter and sweet, with enough body to stand up to milk if desired.
The workhorse matcha in the collection.

photo.alt: Small tin of Marukyu Koyamaen Wako matcha

producer.name: Kanbayashi
producer.history: Tea suppliers to the Tokugawa Shogunate. Centuries-old lineage from Uji.

brewing.notes: Sift before whisking. Use chasen vigorously for 15 seconds until frothy.

notes:
Ceremonial grade matcha from Kanbayashi. Vibrant green, minimal bitterness,
pronounced umami with a clean finish. Best prepared as usucha (thin tea)
for daily ritual. The color is remarkable — electric green.

photo.alt: Tin of Kanbayashi Hikari ceremonial matcha

brewing.notes: Add fresh mint and sugar generously. Pour from height to aerate. The ritual matters.

notes:
Chinese gunpowder green tea base, intended for Moroccan mint tea
preparation. The tea itself is secondary to the ritual — fresh mint,
generous sugar, poured from height into small glasses. Bought in the
Marrakech souk. Might gift to someone who'd enjoy the ritual.

photo.alt: Moroccan mint tea being poured from ornate teapot

producer.name: Yiwu Mountain Tea
producer.history: Yiwu is one of the six ancient tea mountains in Yunnan. Historically a starting point for the ancient tea-horse road.

brewing.notes: Boiling water, very short steeps. Rinse first. This tea will evolve over 10+ steeps. Don't rush it.

notes:
Young sheng pu'er from Yiwu, pressed in 2019. Still evolving — some
bitterness that will mellow with age, but already showing Yiwu's
characteristic honey sweetness and floral notes. Bought to age alongside
the collection. Check back in 2027 for more complexity.

photo.alt: Compressed pu'er cake with wrapper showing Yiwu origin

producer.name: Menghai Tea Factory
producer.history: The original factory that developed the shou (ripe) pu'er process in the 1970s. Benchmark for the style.

brewing.notes: Rinse twice to wake the leaves. Dark, inky liquor. Smooth and grounding.

notes:
Ripe pu'er from Menghai — the factory that invented the accelerated
fermentation process. Earth, dark chocolate, dried dates. Smooth,
thick mouthfeel with no rough edges. A winter evening tea, meditative
and warming. Already well-aged enough to drink now without any pile taste.

photo.alt: Dark compressed shou pu'er brick

producer.name: Marukyu Koyamaen
producer.history: Founded 1704 in Uji. One of the most respected tea houses in Japan, seven generations of expertise.

brewing.notes: First steep at 70°C for umami. Second at 80°C for sweetness. Third at 90°C for astringency and finish.

notes:
Premium sencha from Marukyu Koyamaen. Asatsuyu means "morning dew" — apt for
a tea this clean and refreshing. Vegetal sweetness, light marine notes,
excellent balance. A reliable choice for afternoon sessions.

photo.alt: Green packet of Marukyu Koyamaen Asatsuyu sencha

producer.name: Yame Tea Cooperative
producer.history: Yame region in Fukuoka prefecture, known for premium shaded teas and deep-steamed senchas.

brewing.notes: Deep-steamed leaves break down easily. Use a fine mesh kyusu. Shorter steep than regular sencha.

notes:
Deep-steamed sencha from Yame, Fukuoka. The extended steaming breaks down
leaf structure, producing a richer, more opaque brew with full body and
reduced astringency. Sweet, slightly creamy, with a vegetal depth that
lighter senchas lack. Daily-driver quality.

photo.alt: Deep green fukamushi sencha leaves, finely broken

producer.name: Sonogi Tea Farmers
producer.history: Sonogi in Nagasaki prefecture. Small-scale production influenced by nearby Chinese tea-making, producing a unique curly-leaf green.

brewing.notes: Treat like a sencha but with slightly longer steep. The curly leaves need time to unfurl.

notes:
Tamaryokucha ('curly green tea') from Sonogi — pan-fired rather than
steamed, giving it a rounder, slightly sweeter profile than standard
sencha. The Chinese influence is visible in the curled leaf shape and
the toasty-sweet character. An unusual Japanese green worth exploring.
Currently in tasting mode — deciding if it earns a permanent spot.

photo.alt: Curled green tamaryokucha leaves

producer.name: Jinjiang Yuan
producer.history: Small family producer in Anxi county, Fujian. Three generations of oolong craft.

brewing.notes: Gongfu style. Short steeps, many rounds. The tea evolves beautifully across infusions — floral to mineral to sweet.

notes:
Classic Tieguanyin from Anxi, lightly oxidized (jade style). Orchid
fragrance, clean floral sweetness, with a lingering mineral finish.
The rolled leaves unfurl slowly across steeps, releasing new dimensions.
A great introduction to Chinese oolong tradition.

photo.alt: Tightly rolled green Tieguanyin oolong balls

producer.name: Çaykur
producer.history: State tea enterprise of Turkey, founded 1947. Sources from the steep hillside gardens of Rize province on the Black Sea coast.

brewing.notes: Traditional double-teapot method: strong concentrate on top, diluted with water from below. Served in tulip glasses.

notes:
Turkish black tea from Rize, the heartland of Turkish tea culture.
Strong, tannic, meant to be sipped throughout the day from small tulip
glasses. Not subtle — bold and functional. Pairs with sugar cubes
and conversation. A cultural experience more than a connoisseur tea.

photo.alt: Tulip glass of dark Turkish tea with sugar cubes
